title: SEGMENT 11 — From cells to rats — the abbreviated preclinical safety program

lines.text: SEGMENT 11 — From cells to rats — the abbreviated preclinical safety program
A standard drug, going from in-vitro validation to first-in-human dosing, normally undergoes a years-long preclinical safety program.
Multiple animal species.
Long-term dosing studies.
Toxicology at multiple doses.
Carcinogenicity studies.
Reproductive toxicity studies.
Pharmacokinetic studies in every relevant species.
Histopathology of every organ.
The total preclinical package for a typical IND submission to the FDA is often hundreds of pages and represents years of work and millions of dollars of laboratory effort.
The purpose of all this is to characterize, before you put the drug into a person for the first time, every reasonably foreseeable risk the drug poses.
In milasen's case, there was no time for any of that.
Mila was already having thirty seizures a day.
Her brain was atrophying.
Every month that passed was a month of continuing neurodegeneration that the drug, even if it worked perfectly when it arrived, could not undo.
The team and the FDA had to find a way to characterize the drug's safety just enough to justify intrathecal dosing in a child, in a fraction of the time a normal preclinical program would take.
What they did, and what the FDA accepted, was a single abbreviated rat toxicology study, and they leaned heavily on the safety record of nusinersen — the precedent drug — for everything else.

Because milasen had the same chemistry, the same backbone, the same general structural class, and the same delivery route as nusinersen, and nusinersen had been dosed in many hundreds of children by 2017 with an excellent safety profile, the regulatory argument was that the major safety questions about the platform itself had already been answered.

The only question that needed fresh data, for milasen specifically, was whether this particular sequence — when administered intrathecally — caused any acute, dose-dependent toxicity that would change the calculus for first-in-human dosing.

In December of 2017, the team administered milasen to rats by intrathecal injection — exactly the same route that would eventually be used in Mila — at three escalating doses: 0.06 milligrams, 0.25 milligrams, and 1.0 milligram per animal.
Those doses were chosen to span and exceed the maximum doses that had been used in nusinersen clinical trials, on a body-weight basis.
After dosing, the animals were observed for behavioral and neurological signs, and then sacrificed at intervals for tissue analysis.
The 0.06-milligram dose was uneventful.
The animals showed no acute neurological signs and no histological abnormalities in the spinal cord or brain.

The 0.25-milligram and 1.0-milligram doses produced a transient hindlimb weakness in some animals — the rats had some difficulty moving their back legs for a few hours after the injection — but the weakness resolved by about twenty-four hours, and no permanent damage was seen.

The pattern of transient hindlimb weakness was, importantly, exactly the kind of finding that had been reported in preclinical studies of nusinersen at high doses — it appeared to be a consequence of bolus intrathecal injection of any phosphorothioate ASO, not something specific to milasen.
So while it set an upper bound on the dose that could be considered safe, it did not raise novel safety concerns.
On the basis of this study, plus the platform safety data from nusinersen, the team and the FDA agreed on a clinical dosing strategy.
Start at a low dose, well below any dose that had caused observable toxicity in the rat study.
Escalate gradually, over multiple administrations, monitoring carefully for any adverse effects.

The target maintenance dose was set at 42 milligrams in Mila — a dose that, in terms of the molar amount of oligonucleotide delivered to the CSF, was equivalent to about three times the standard clinical dose of nusinersen.

The rationale for choosing three times higher was that antisense oligonucleotides distribute differently within the central nervous system depending on the route and rate of delivery, and the team estimated, based on existing pharmacokinetic data, that milasen would distribute about one-third as well to the brain as to the spinal cord.

So a dose three times higher than nusinersen would, by their estimate, deliver to the brain roughly the same molar exposure that nusinersen achieves in the spinal cord, where its primary site of action is.
I want to flag something important about the preclinical program here, because if you go on to work in drug development you will encounter strong reactions to it from both sides.
On one hand, the rat study was abbreviated.

It was a single species, a single short-duration study, a small number of animals, with no long-term toxicology, no reproductive studies, no carcinogenicity work, no detailed pharmacokinetic profiling.
By the standards of a normal IND, this is a small fraction of the preclinical package a regulator would normally demand before allowing first-in-human dosing.
On the other hand — and this is the argument the FDA accepted, in the specific context of this specific patient — the chemistry was not novel.
The delivery route was not novel.
The molecular class was not novel.
The patient was facing a rapidly fatal disease with no other treatment options.

The reasonable risk of an unexpected adverse effect from a new sequence, when the underlying platform had been extensively characterized in another approved drug, was judged to be low relative to the certain harm of withholding any attempt at treatment.
So the abbreviated program was, in the FDA's judgment, justified for this particular case under the Expanded Access regulatory pathway — which we'll discuss next.
I want you to notice the structure of this decision.
The preclinical program was not abbreviated by lowering the safety bar.
It was abbreviated by inheriting safety data from a closely related precedent drug.
That distinction is important.
Milasen's safety case rested heavily on the fact that the platform — the chemistry, the delivery route, the molecular class — had already been characterized for nusinersen.
Without that precedent, no abbreviated rat study would have been sufficient.
The platform's prior validation was the safety case.
The rat study was a final, narrow confirmation that this specific sequence did not introduce some new, unexpected, sequence-specific toxicity.
This is the model that has now been adopted, more or less, by the field for individualized ASO therapies.
You can move fast for individual patients, but only because the platform you are using has already been extensively safety-characterized by precedent drugs.
The first drug in any new class will always require a full preclinical program.
Subsequent drugs in the same class, with the same chemistry and delivery, can be developed much more quickly.

Milasen was the first published example of this principle being applied to a brand-new drug for a single patient — and the speed of its development is a direct demonstration of what platform inheritance can buy you.
